data.title: China Likely to Start Bolstering Trade Defenses Ahead of NegotiationsMarket Talk
data.description: China is likely to start bolstering trade defenses ahead of negotiations, Macquarie analyst Eugene Hsiao writes in a note. Trump's new tariff announcements signal that now is the real starting point for trade negotiations, he says. While the potential shock to China's economy has already been widely discussed, markets remain in a holding pattern on discounting the real impact to growth until more clarity is reached, he says. China's recent tech re-rating is mostly insulated from tariffs, he says, adding that investment focus now returns to potential domestic stimulus to boost consumption to offset tariff impact. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Market Chatter: Growing Uncertainty from US tariffs to Lead to More Dovish Reserve Bank, Citi says
data.description:
The growing uncertainty caused by the 10% "reciprocal tariffs" imposed by the US on Australia may push the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) towards a more dovish stance in the near term, according to a Thursday report by the Australian Financial Review, citing Citi.

Citi expects the RBA will cut the cash rate next month and again in August, reducing the benchmark rate to 3.6 percent, per the report.

However, Citi is keeping the country's growth forecast unchanged, noting that the 10% tariffs are unlikely to derail the economy's recovery this year, the report added.

data.title: Indian chemical makers drop on US reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
** Shares of Indian chemical makers SRF , UPL drop 5.1% and 1.6%, respectively, while Navin Fluorine plunged 5.4%

** Deepak Nitrite down 2.4%

** U.S. President Donald Trump imposed 26% reciprocal tariffs on Indian imports as part of his wider plan to levy a 10% baseline tariff on all imports from April 5

** Sectors including chemicals likely to take major hit from the broader tariffs, analysts at Nirmal Bang said in a note

** Chemicals account for about 4.01% of India's exports share to the U.S. - Nirmal Bang

** SRFL, DPNT rated "hold" on avg, UPL, NAFL rated "Buy" - data compiled by LSEG

** Year-to-date, SRFL, UPLL, NAFL, up 33%, ~31%, ~31%, while DPNT down ~19%

data.title: Hardest-Hit Stocks in Asia: Tech, Japanese Banks and a Nike Supplier
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Asian stocks were down across the board in Thursday trading after President Trump released his tariff plan. Japan's Nikkei Stock Average was down 3% and the VN index in Vietnam-a country that would be subject to some of the highest levies under Trump's plan-fell 6%. Here are the hardest-hit areas:

-Technology: Many of the electronics and home appliances Americans buy are manufactured in East Asia, so tariffs will hit companies in those businesses. Hong Kong-listed shares of Chinese computer maker Lenovo Group fell 7.5%, South Korea's LG Electronics was down 5.7% and Japanese memory-chip maker Kioxia fell 11%.

-Japanese Banks: The country's three biggest banks were each down more than 7.8% on expectations of lower lending rates and bond yields as the Bank of Japan and the Federal Reserve brace for a possible global recession. Lower rates could hit banks' margins.

-Apparel: Shenzhou International Group, a supplier for global apparel brands including Nike and Uniqlo, fell 14% in Hong Kong.

Looking for a bright spot? Markets in China and South Korea fell only slightly. And Indian drugmakers rose because Trump didn't announce pharmaceutical tariffs.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Malaysia Seeks Fair Trade Talks with U.S. After Tariff Hike
data.description:
Malaysia is in discussions with U.S. officials to ensure equitable trade terms following President Trump’s new tariffs, including a 10% universal import levy and a 24% reciprocal duty on Malaysian goods.

The Ministry of Investment, Trade & Industry (MITI) recognized the tariffs as disruptive but stressed Malaysia’s focus on maintaining open trade rather than retaliating.

MITI reiterated its intention to protecting national economic interests while reinforcing U.S. trade relations
As the U.S.’s 15th-largest trading partner in 2024, Malaysia logged a USD 24.8 billion trade surplus with America.

While the U.S. faces a goods deficit with Malaysia, it retains a services surplus, bolstered by decades of U.S. corporate presence in the country.

Malaysia plans to push for mutual trade benefits and a potential technology pact to deepen collaboration in semiconductors, aerospace, and digital industries.

data.title: Wall Street Journal - April 3
data.description:
The following are the top stories in the Wall Street Journal. Reuters has not verified these stories and does not vouch for their accuracy.

- Mark Zuckerberg is lobbying U.S. President Donald Trump and White House officials to agree to a settlement that would prevent Meta Platforms from facing an antitrust trial later this month.

- Mobile technology company AppLovin has made a bid for TikTok and talked to casino magnate Steve Wynn about backing it.

- Siemens has agreed to acquire Dotmatics for $5.1 billion, a combination the companies said would use artificial intelligence to speed up development cycles and help make breakthroughs in the life-sciences space.

- The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) put on hold a lawsuit against pharmacy-benefit manager (PBM) units of UnitedHealth , Cigna and CVS Health over insulin prices after the Trump administration fired two of the agency's commissioners.

- The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has missed the deadline for making a key decision regarding a COVID-19 vaccine from Novavax , days after the Food and Drug Administration's vaccine chief was pushed out.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 1 AM ET
data.description:
Asian Markets Roiled by 'Liberation Day' Tariffs

Asian equities tumbled as markets awoke to see some of their worst fears come true: a slate of sweeping U.S. tariffs that could inflict economic damage and disrupt industries.

Trump Tariffs Aim to Bring Down Curtain on Era of Globalization

The president wants companies to return production to the U.S., but it won't be easy.

Trump's Tariffs 'A Major Blow to the World Economy,' EU's Von der Leyen Says

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said it is not too late to engage in talks with the U.S.

China Services Gauge Shows Acceleration in Activity Expansion

China's services-sector activity expanded at a faster pace in March, thanks to improving demand boosted by government stimulus measures, according to a private survey.

Trump Officials Gave Conflicting Tariff Rates for China. White House Calls the Issue a 'Pointless Aside.'

Confusion reigned even among some White House officials about what rate the approximately $440 billion in Chinese imports would face.

Some GOP Senators Break With Trump to Reject Canada Tariffs

The Senate passed a resolution to undo the emergency declaration the president used to impose the tariffs, but the measure has been blocked in the Republican-controlled House.

What to Know About Trump's Latest Tariffs

President Trump unveiled a new tariff regime, including 10% across-the-board tariffs on all imports and even higher rates for some nations. Here's the latest.

The Stock Market Pain Is Just Getting Started. 'This is How You Sabotage the World's Economic Engine.'

"Liberation Day" wasn't the clearing event investors hopped for. That isn't good news for stocks-in the short run.

Trump's tariffs are 'a game changer,' not only for U.S. economy but for the world

President Donald Trump's new tariff plan is a "tsunami" that will slam into the global economy and have repercussions that are hard to quantify, economists said Wednesday.

Jobs Aren't a Worry-Yet. Tariffs and DOGE Could Upset Things.

Businesses and corporate budgets are under pressure amid uncertainty over President Donald Trump's tariff hikes and federal job cuts.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 1 AM ET
data.description:
AppLovin and Amazon Emerge as TikTok Bidders Ahead of Trump's Deadline

President Trump was briefed Wednesday on a framework to keep the video-sharing app operational.

Zuckerberg Lobbies Trump to Avoid Meta Antitrust Trial

Meta's chief executive was at the White House on Wednesday, his third visit during Trump's presidency, according to people familiar with the matter.

Apple's stock is getting pounded on tariff news. Don't freak out just yet.

Apple has a loyal customer base, strong gross margins - and a history of getting tariff exemptions.

RH's stock slammed by new tariffs and outlook, but CEO offers this praise for Trump administration

Shares of RH fell sharply after hours on Wednesday after the luxury furniture chain's full-year sales outlook came in shy of expectations, amid a forecast of what it characterized as a "higher-risk business environment this year" due to tariffs, seesawing markets and inflation.

Venezuela Bondholders Find Path to Repayment in Citgo Auction

The leading bid for Citgo favors creditors whose collateral rights over the refiner are in dispute.

Macy's $150 Million Accounting Error Wrongly Boosted Executives' Pay

The company erroneously overpaid C-suite members by about $600,000 in 2023 as a result of an accounting error that hid $150 million in delivery costs.

Tesla's Global Vehicle Deliveries Sank 13% in First Quarter

The decline is further evidence that a growing consumer backlash against the brand and CEO Elon Musk is hurting the automaker's business.

Siemens Agrees to Buy Dotmatics for $5.1 Billion, Expanding AI Portfolio to Life Sciences

The deal extends Siemens's artificial-intelligence-powered product-lifecycle-management portfolio and increases the company's total addressable market in industrial software by $11 billion.

Hims & Hers Zepbound Offering Won't Bring Needed Sales Boom, Analysts Say

Investors worry patients won't make that price leap and that Hims, which has built its business on affordable healthcare, will struggle to meet its revenue goal.

Trump Media Shares Sink After Clearing Path for President's Trust to Sell Stake

The stock was recently headed for its lowest close of the year.

data.title: Amcor's Bloom Biorenewables Secures $14 Million Series A Funding
data.description:
Amcor said Bloom Biorenewables, a company that it's invested in secured over $14 million in Series A funding, according to a Thursday statement.

The funding was led by Anaïs Ventures and Valquest Partners, joined by other venture capital firms and strategic investors, including Amcor.

The company has been collaborating with Bloom on research and development projects in more sustainable plastics, aiming to advance packaging products with a higher share of bio-based content that meets sustainability standards.

data.title: Muthoot Finance to issue multiple tenor bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's Muthoot Finance plans to raise 30 billion rupees ($350.21 million) through the sale of bonds maturing in three years, as well as in three years and 49 days, three bankers said on Thursday.

The non-banking finance company will pay an annual coupon of 8.52% on each issue and has invited bids from bankers and investors on Friday, they said.

The company did not reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.

data.title: NobleOak Life Engages with Victorian Authorities to Determine Impact from Change in Regulations
data.description:
NobleOak Life said it is engaging with the State Revenue Office of Victoria to determine the potential application of the amendments to the Victorian Duties Act 2000 for applicable insurance contracts in Victoria from Jan. 1, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

It currently pays stamp duty on relevant insurance policies in all Australian states except Victoria due to its status as a friendly society.

The recent changes amend the stamp duty exemptions that were previously available to friendly societies for insurance contracts insuring residents of Victoria. Such changes generally do not apply to death benefit premiums.

Its shares fell 2% on market close on Thursday.

data.title: Yuan Weakens, Dragged by Trump's Tariff AnnouncementMarket Talk
data.description: The Chinese yuan weakens against the dollar in offshore and onshore markets, dragged by U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement overnight. Emerging-market Asian currencies took a hit from the reciprocal tariffs, Mizuho Securities Asia's Ken Cheung says in an email. Several Asian countries such as Vietnam, Thailand, and Taiwan are facing significant reciprocal tariff increases, the director of FX Strategy says. "Looking ahead, we reckon that the PBOC will allow gradual CNY fixing weakening to introduce more two-way market flexibility to adjust to choppy market conditions after the US tariff day," Cheung adds. USD/CNY is 0.4% higher at 7.2983; USD/CNH is up 0.2% at 7.3114. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: China's Export-Driven Companies Could Face Challenges After Trump's TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: China's export-driven companies will face challenges after President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, either in the form of lower margins or reduced sales volumes if they pass on the additional costs to customers, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su says. Weaker exports could further slow China's economic growth and in turn dampen domestic consumption, he adds. "This broader economic slowdown could create a ripple effect, ultimately impacting even domestically-oriented companies," he adds. Su notes that the key factor is how much additional stimulus the Chinese government is prepared to implement to offset the tariff-induced economic slowdown. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Jungfraubahn Logs Lower FY24 Profit; Operating Income Up
data.description:
Jungfraubahn said Thursday that profit for the full-year 2024 decreased, while operating income increased year over year.

Profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 76.5 million francs, compared with 79.6 million francs earlier.

The Swiss cogwheel railway and winter sports-related facilities operator's operating income was 294.7 million francs, compared with 278.1 million francs a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a dividend of 7.50 francs per share for the period. It paid 6.50 francs per share a year ago.

data.title: Indian IT stocks fall on worries over impact to U.S. economy from Trump tariffs
data.description:
** Indian IT shares down 3.5%

** Index is top sectoral loser among Nifty indexes

** U.S. President Donald Trump slapped a bigger-than-expected wall of tariffs around the world's largest economy, possibly impacting trade and supply chains

** Indian IT companies may see indirect impacts from slower U.S. GDP growth due to higher tariffs, impacting demand from manufacturing, logistics and retail verticals despite U.S. rate cuts - Jefferies

** Revenue growth for IT firms is highly correlated to U.S. real GDP growth - brokerage

** Among large-caps, Tech Mahindra , HCLTech have strong exposure to manufacturing, retail verticals in U.S. - Jefferies

** The two companies decline 2.7% and 2.5%, respectively

** Persistent Systems down the most by 8.5%, Coforge loses 6.5%

** YTD, IT stocks have fallen 19% vs 1.7% decline in Nifty 50 index

data.title: Clarification regarding the United Camping Group's acquisition of Apukka Resort
data.description:
First Camp Group AB

As communicated separately, a sister company to First Camp Group is acquiring 100% of the shares in Apukka Resort, a leading player in the tourism industry in Finnish Lapland. The acquiring company is outside of First Camp Group AB and its subsidiaries. Therefore, bondholders in First Camp Group AB are not directly affected by this transaction.

Apukka Resort, located just outside Rovaniemi, has an annual turnover of approximately 22 million EUR and the business includes 116 cabins and glass igloos. Winter tourism in Rovaniemi is experiencing strong growth, and there are currently over 40 direct flight routes to and from Rovaniemi.  

Closing is expected to take place during the second quarter of 2025.

For more information, please contact: 

Göran Meijer 

CFO United Camping-koncernen 

goran.meijer@firstcamp.se

Johan Söör 

VD United Camping-koncernen och First Camp 

johan.soor@firstcamp.se

About us

First Camp is Scandinavia's largest camping chain, with over 70 destinations ranging from Boden in northern Sweden to Frigård in southern Denmark, including four destinations in Norway. The brands Jesperhus Feriepark, Leksand Resort, and Kronocamping Lidköping are also part of the group. First Camp's heart beats for everyone who loves staying in cabins, caravans, motorhomes, or tents, with approximately six million guest overnight stays annually. The company is passionate about offering scenic and beautiful locations and carefully designed experiences. First Camp aims to become the world's leading camping chain through a clear focus on innovation and industry development, investments in sustainability, and, of course, exceptionally satisfied guests.

data.title: Australia PM Questions 29% Tariff on Tiny Norfolk IslandMarket Talk
data.description: While Australia faces a 10% duty on sales to the U.S. under President Trump's sweeping tariff plan, tiny Norfolk Island--an external Australian territory--faces a tariff of 29%, a decision that's perplexing Australian officials. "We regard the Norfolk Island decision as a rather strange one," says Prime Minister Anthony Albanese. "It, of course, is a part of Australia" but "with a 29% tariff imposed." Australia's government will be seeking an explanation from the Trump administration, he says. "I'm not sure that Norfolk Island, with respect to it, is a threat to the United States economy, and therefore, I think it being singled out is rather strange," Albanese says. Norfolk Island had a population of 2,188 people in a 2021 census. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Spain Backs Openness, PM Sanchez Says After Trump Unveils Europe TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: Spain will keep fighting for an open world, premier Pedro Sanchez insists as the EU braces for 20% tariffs on its exports to the U.S., the bloc's most important trading partner. The trade duties, part of a major package set out Wednesday by President Trump, could hamstring exporters and depress Europe's growth. "Spain will protect its companies and its workers and will continue to back openness in the world," Sanchez says on X following a meeting with employer and worker associations.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)
